attributes.stem:
A 38-year-old woman presents with an eight hour history of sudden onset of vomiting, dysphagia and severe epigastric discomfort. She has had several similar episodes over the past month, but none as severe as this one. Ten years ago she had undergone a gastric banding operation for obesity, but does not have any other current health issues. Recently, she thinks she may have been eating more than normally.

On examination, her blood pressure is 132/90 mmHg, pulse 104/min and temperature 37.2C. Examination of her abdomen is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
This woman is likely experiencing restrictive symptoms (vomiting, dysphagia and epigastric discomfort) from her gastric band being overfilled during an adjustment. The resulting tachycardia is likely due to ongoing vomiting. Post-operatively, individuals may require gastric band adjustments in order to find the optimal fluid volume alongside their diet. Increased pressures from over-eating as well as regurgitation and vomiting are also important in individuals with a gastric band as these increased pressure may contribute to gastric band slippage. The most appropriate next step would be to withdraw fluid from the band. This is a relatively simple approach and will immediately indicate whether symptoms are related to the gastric band.

Given the ongoing vomiting and dysphagia, a barium swallow is unlikely to be a viable option. It is also less likely to be an oesophageal issue given that symptoms have not been consistent over the previous months.

A plain abdominal X-ray may be useful in circumstances of bowel obstruction or gastrointestinal perforation, however given the abdominal findings are unremarkable, this is unlikely to be necessary.

An abdominal ultrasound would likely be the most viable option if the patient had not had a gastric band. Abdominal ultrasound is a useful tool for suspected pancreatitis, as well as gallbladder and biliary tree related pathology. It can be used at the bedside and is relatively inexpensive. A CT abdomen can be used for similar pathology (suspected pancreatitis) but is generally not recommended within 48 hours of seeing a patient unless there is clinical concern or diagnostic doubt.
learningPoints: Individuals with gastric bands require ongoing adjustment post-operatively in order to find the optimal fluid volume alongside their regular diet
An abdominal ultrasound is a suitable tool in assessing for pancreatitis and generally the first modality of imaging for pancreatitis when compared to CT
references: Flint RS, Coulter G, Roberts R. The pattern of adjustments after laparoscopic adjustable gastric band. Obesity Surgery. 2015;25(11):2061–5.

Brown WA, Burton PR, Anderson M, Korin A, Dixon JB, Hebbard G, et al. Symmetrical pouch dilatation after laparoscopic adjustable gastric banding: Incidence and management. Obesity Surgery. 2008;18(9):1104–8.
Goodchild G, Chouhan M, Johnson GJ. Practical guide to the management of acute pancreatitis. Frontline Gastroenterology. 2019;10(3):292–9.
